data.title: Forrester Research Q4: A Business Turnaround That Is Taking Its Time
data.description:
Investment thesis

In my previous write-up covering Forrester Research (NASDAQ:FORR), I had assigned the stock a Hold rating, stating that caution was warranted until there were clear signs of revenue stabilization. While revenue in Q4 declined 9% year over year, certain key underlying metrics are trending in the right direction, with management expecting stabilization in bookings for 2025. While shares remain cheap at just 7.5 times earnings, I am still remaining cautious until there is further clarity regarding the outlook and execution risks are no longer a concern. As a result, I am maintaining my Hold rating on FORR stock.

Underwhelming results caused by multiple factors

Forrester's Q4 results showed a 9% year-over-year decline in total revenue, resulting from year-over-year declines in all its three business segments, namely Research, Consulting and Event, as shown above. Revenue from the Research segment, being the largest and also the most significant as it mainly depends on the Forrester Decisions ("FD") product, which is the company's flagship platform product offering, was down 7% year over year.

While some of this weakness was attributed to lower spending by clients in the technology sector, a bigger issue facing the company is that it has lost a lot of clients, especially smaller ones. According to its latest quarterly report, the total number of clients stood at 1,942 which was down 14% year over year and down 3% sequentially. While the company's sales organization has instead focused on added larger clients, they have faced more complex sales cycles and more execution risks.

Despite the lower revenue in Q4, operating income increased by 32% to $8.9 million due to strict cost controls which included workforce reductions, as operating expenses fell to $108.6 million, down 9% year over year. While total revenue in FY24 was down 10%, adjusted earnings fell 27% versus the prior year, to $1.47 per share.

Improving underlying metrics point to stability ahead

While Q4 results were weak, certain underlying metrics have been improving. For instance, wallet retention improved to 89% compared to 87% in the prior year period, as shown in the figure above. This has coincided with the strong growth in FD, which is increasingly accounting for a larger portion of the overall Contract Value ("CV").

During the earnings call management stated that the percentage of multi-year client contracts grew from 62% to 69%, while also stating that clients heavily using Izola, Forrester's generative AI tool which helps in retrieving answers from the research database, were renewing at 20% higher rates.

While management has guided for revenue to be down between 4% to 8% in FY25, it is important to note that this reflects the lower bookings that occurred during FY24. Instead, the leading indicator that investors should focus on is the CV, which is more aligned with bookings in 2025, which management expects this to be flat year over year. Notably, as shown in the earlier figure, while CV was down 5% year over year in Q4, it was only down 0.8% compared to Q3 2024, indicating that there are early signs of stability in this value. Current market forecasts see the Technology Advisory Market growing 4.2% in 2025, with an uptick to a 5.2% CAGR between 2026 and 2029, which should provide tailwinds for future demand.

With the mid-single digit revenue decline expected, as well as higher investments expected related to driving demand through more Forrester Events, adjusted earnings for FY25 are expected to be $1.28 per share at the midpoint, down 13% year over year.

Cheap valuation not enough to compensate for the high uncertainty

Based on management's guidance for FY25 adjusted earnings, FORR stock is currently trading at an earnings multiple of 10.3. If we adjust for the company's $69 million net cash position, which equates to $3.6 per share, its valuation multiple drops to just 7.5. This makes shares look very cheap, especially with its closest public market peer Gartner (IT) trading at well over 30 times earnings.

On the flip side, one aspect that investors have to consider is the high stock-based compensation expenses, which is expected to be $15 million or $0.79 per share, at the midpoint for FY25. This represents a large portion of FORR's adjusted earnings, which makes its GAAP valuation significantly higher.

According to me, FORR's well-recognized brand coupled with its strong balance sheet and expectation for generating positive FCF in the coming year, which supports the ongoing share repurchases, should provide reasonable downside support for its share price. However, the biggest risk going forward stems from execution risks, especially related to the sales of the FD product. Investors ought to closely track the metrics which I highlighted above to ensure that management is finding success in driving growth through FD.

While the number of smaller clients has come down in recent years, FORR does still have exposure to these customers, who could in particular could pull back on spending if macroeconomic pressures worsen from here.

Conclusion

While wallet retention is improving and CV is showing signs of stabilization, with management expecting CV to be flat year over year, I am remaining cautious despite the cheap valuation. I will be tracking these key metrics going forward in order to judge if this turnaround story is on the right track.

data.title: Canadian Tire: Tariffs Cloud Outlook
data.description:
Canadian Tire (CTC.A:CA) (OTCPK:CDNAF) is an iconic brand in Canada with offerings across household goods, automotive and sportswear. I have covered the CTC story as the company has navigated through challenging times. You can read my in-depth introduction into the business segments here, and performance updates here and here.

In a nutshell, my thesis has been that Canadian Tire is a great brand and company in the Canadian market, and offers good value compared to peers. However, CTC is so tied in to discretionary spending in the Canadian economy, that their short-term performance is likely to be challenged. I have consistently rated CTC as a Hold.

To be fair, management has been doing a great job of, in their words “controlling the controllable” and navigating through the recent spending softness. They have cut costs, invested in brand loyalty, and run some effective sales promotions.

However, a ‘Trump’eting elephant has barged into the mall - with the recent threat of 25% tariffs from the U.S. This has created a lot of uncertainty in Canada, and as a result, in the outlook for CTC.

The company reported FY 24 earnings on Feb 13th, the stock dropped around 6% on the day.

Let’s have a look at how they have been doing, and what they say about the outlook.

FY 2024 Results

Overall, the results announced on Feb 13th were pretty decent.

Comparable sales for the quarter were up, albeit a modest 1.1%. This top line performance, while below the rate of inflation, reverses a trend of declining sales for the first part of the year.

Full year numbers show a 21% increase in Earnings Per Share year, after normalising for some significant charges in 2023, and one off benefits in 2024.

2023 saw charges for restructuring and headcount reductions, and someone off accounting charges, plus the impacts of a large fire.

2024 tailwinds included some property sales, plus insurance recoveries related to the major fire.

While it makes for very lumpy earnings, the normalising items seem reasonable.

However, these figures represent a slight miss versus analyst expectations, as shown by Seeking Alpha. Normalised EPS came in 5% below expectations, and revenue around 1.5%.

This implies that analysts had expected CTC to deliver higher margins than were achieved. This makes sense to me, as there is evidence of price discounting in order to keep sales up in the face of consumer spending softness.

All in all, the full year results show a lackluster year, with a contraction of 1.8% in retail sales, and a 1.2% drop in diluted EPS.

Share Price Reaction

The market did not like the figures. The share price fell by as much as 8% post earnings, recovering slightly to a 7.4% drop, in a flat Canadian market (TSX).

Outlook & The Tariff Elephant

Clearly the performance figures alone don't justify such a drop. The main cause would seem to be the business outlook.

Greg Hicks, the CEO made some pretty sombre comments on the earnings call in relation to the potential U.S. tariffs. While the direct impacts seem to be controllable, the outlook for the Canadian consumer seems to be more of a challenge to CTC.

On the positive side, the impact of the BOC rate reductions in the latter part of 2024 were felt in terms of increased consumer demand, as was a more vibrant real estate market. CTC products are in demand for folks setting up new households.

However, Hicks referenced the continued overhang of impending mortgage rate resets in 2025, with an estimate of 1.2m mortgages due for refinancing in 2025. These are typically five year re-set mortgages, so consumers would be facing significant increases in shelter costs even after the recent BOC rate cuts.

More critically, Hicks addressed the potential impact of tariffs explicitly on the call.

Now, I'd be remiss if I didn't caveat this optimism with the looming threat of tariffs. I suspect the consumer confidence uptick that I mentioned earlier has now been substantially erased with tariff talk, while that threat may be on pause, we are conducting all the business assessments and preparations you would expect.

And later in response to an analyst question on the tariff impact potential:

Yes, I mean, the situation is so unfortunate on so many levels at a time when the economy seemed to be moving in the right direction, and we were gearing up for a return to growth. We're now faced with the threat of tariffs. And as you suggest, a new zone of uncertainty.

In concrete terms, Hicks highlighted the broad potential impact on the Canadian consumer, reducing product demand. Furthermore, he raised the possibility of write-offs for bad loans at the financing arm. In my view, this is probably the aspect that it of most concern. Fluctuations in demand can be managed by inventory, margin and cost management, which are, to a certain degree, controllable.

For their operations, 15% of goods are currently purchased from the U.S., and CTC has currency hedges in place. Furthermore, Canadian Tire is likely to benefit from a 'Buy Canadian' movement against U.S. competitors in most of its operating segments.

Bad debts, however, are hard to protect against, especially for consumer credit.

The loan provisions on the books are modelled using a 6.6% unemployment rate, the December 2024 level, with an implicit assumption that the employment situation will improve into 2025. A recent RBC report on tariff impact projected a 25% tariff would increase unemployment to 8%. Hicks implied that a higher unemployment rate implies a higher delinquency rate which would fall straight to the bottom line. Again, I think it's important to hear how he worded this.

So any movement up from that exit rate is going to impact the ECL and most likely write-offs in the year. So those become a little bit harder for us to manage. It's more of a straight line, unfortunately.

ECL stands for 'Expected Credit Losses'. ECL provisions at year-end were $935m, which is equivalent to the whole of the 2024 net income.

In section 6 of the MD&A document I found the following exhibit, which puts loans receivable at $6.7bn. So the current ECL is around 14% of loans receivable.

I don't have an economic model for loan delinquency rates at different unemployment levels, however I would assume that the relationship is non-linear, but rather exponential.

It seems safe to assume that there will be some form of tariffs beyond what is currently in place, and that these will have a negative impact on employment in Canada.

Even a base case scenario would imply a double-digit increase in ECL provisions, say $100m or 10% of net income - worst-case scenarios ratcheting up from there.

It is no wonder that Hicks seemed somewhat rattled on the call, or that the share price dropped.

So where does that lead us?

Valuation

CTC has consistently been cheap when compared to peers like Home Depot and Walmart. While these are not totally direct comparisons, there is enough of an overlap to make CTC look like a bargain.

Consider price to earnings:

And price to free cash flow:

On both metrics, CTC is very cheap by comparison. But also in absolute terms, at 14x earnings, and under 9x FCF, the stock looks investable. CTC also pays a dividend which is around 5% at current prices, so hits the screens for income investors.

The question is, with the economic concentration risk enhanced by current tariff uncertainty, does the current price justify the risk?

Conclusion & My Own Positioning

  • Clearly the market sees a significant risk potential to the forward earnings.
  • Management has been doing a decent job with the cards that they have been dealt.
  • The actual performance has been OK, and there is light at the end of the tunnel.
  • Tariffs look to have the potential to extend the journey back to growth, and potentially create a significant earnings shock.
  • For me, Canadian Tire remains a long-term hold due to its strong franchise, clear strategy, and sound management.
  • I see the current risk landscape as enhanced, and investors who actively trade might want to step to the sidelines here.

As discussed in previous articles, I like Canadian Tire as a business, and want to find a place for it in my portfolio. However, I have traded out when the price has run up too much in the past. I have been a buyer at around $135, sold when the price ran up to $200, and bought again at $135.

For full disclosure, when Trump made the 25% tariff announcement at the beginning of February I sold my position at $158. I then sold $135 puts on Feb 13th. I remain comfortable owning Canadian Tire with a sub $135 cost base.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: FreightCar America: Buffeted By Tariff Concerns
data.description:
Shares of railcar manufacturer FreightCar America, Inc. (NASDAQ:RAIL) have fallen by a quarter since its 3Q24 financial report in November, as the market was spooked by a big revenue miss and tariff concerns. Also concerning were industrywide orders that are projected to decline 20% FY24 vs FY23, though the company is picking up market share as its move to a facility in Mexico is nearly complete. Recently, the shares have also been buffeted by increasing concerns around tariffs, which an article here on Seeking Alpha in late January discussed in depth.

With ~250,000 railcars compelled to exit service from FY24 to FY28 and FreightCar securing its first major tank car order, the recent insider buying merited a deeper dive. FreightCar America will be entering out model portfolio as of today. A full investment analysis and recommendation follows below.

Company Overview

FreightCar America, Inc. is a Chicago based pure-play manufacturer of aluminum and steel railcars and components to shippers, railroads, and financial institutions (lessors) in the U.S. The company provides box cars, covered and open-top hoppers, gondolas, intermodal, non-intermodal flat cars, and most recently tank cars out of its manufacturing campus in Castanos, Coahuila located in Northern Mexico. With roots dating back to 1901, FreightCar went public in 2005, raising net proceeds of $85.3 million at $19 per share. Its stock currently trades at ten bucks a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of approximately $210 million.

Migration to Mexico

When the company went public it had four manufacturing facilities in the U.S. After the economic collapse in 2008, FreightCar struggled to recover over the ensuing decade, eventually shuttering its plant in Roanoke, Virginia in 2017, divesting of its Danville, Illinois manufacturing facility in 2018, and suspending operations at its Cherokee, Alabama yard in early 2021. The company’s remaining American property (outside its Chicago headquarters) in Johnstown, Pennsylvania is now used to produce aftermarket parts.

FreightCar moved into its Castanos campus during 3Q20 and has expanded its footprint by adding a fourth assembly line in 2024. As currently configured, it is a vertically integrated facility (fabrication, assembly, finishing, and inspection), employing ~2,000 individuals with capacity to manufacture or overhaul over 5,000 railcars each year, encompassing 45 different designs.

FreightCar commands a ~10% share of the ~40,000 unit North American railcar market while – up until recently – servicing only 60% of it. With the continuing expansion of its Mexican facility, it now has the capacity to grow its total addressable market by an additional 20%, entering tank car production after securing a two-year, 1,000+ unit conversion order in 3Q24.

Operating Performance

Beset by an underfunded pension and unable to attain profitability on a non-GAAP basis since 2016 (when it delivered 5,559 railcars and revenue of $523.7 million), the goal of the company’s migration to Mexico was to become more competitive through cheaper rent and labor, allowing it to prevail in mid- and up cycles if they were to ever reoccur. Featuring more flexible assembly lines, lower production expenses per railcar, and an exit from its leasing business – meaning FreightCar was no longer competing with a significant portion of its client base – the transformation made the company more competitive.

After its deliveries, revenue, and stock price bottomed out somewhat artificially in 2020 at 751 railcars, $108.4 million, and $0.73 a share (respectively), FreightCar began to benefit from an upturn in the railcar cycle – initially on unfulfilled demand shut in during the pandemic – achieving deliveries of 3,184 railcars in FY22, which translated to Adj. EBITDA of $8.4 million on revenue of $364.8 million, representing its first positive Adj. EBITDA figure since FY16. The upswing seemed to have stalled in FY23 with deliveries down 5% to 3,022 in FY23 (and down 15% versus management’s initial projection of 3,550), hurt somewhat by the closure of the U.S.-Mexico border in December of that year. Shares of RAIL closed the 2023 campaign at $2.70, down 69% from their (then) post-pandemic high of $8.63 set in 2021.

That said, owing to better pricing and increasing efficiencies at its new facility, FreightCar’s FY23 Adj. EBITDA grew 139% to 20.1 million. Management then forecasted FY24 deliveries of 4,200 and Adj. EBITDA of $35 million on revenue of $546 million, representing improvements of 39%, 74%, and 53% over FY23 (respectively), as part of its 4Q23 financial report. By the time the company’s reported strong 2Q24 financials in August, it had delivered its 10,000th railcar from its new campus, completed the construction of its fourth assembly line, and received its first order for tank cars. Additionally, FreightCar received net orders of 2,916 units in the quarter, representing the most it had received since 4Q14, raising its backlog to 3,833 railcars valued at $382 million – its second-highest level since 2016.

With its production facility sold out for the balance of the year and clearly gaining market share, management raised estimates for deliveries by 7% to 4,500, Adj. EBITDA by 6% to $37 million, and revenue by 6% to $580 million. These developments sparked a 17% rally in the subsequent trading session and a three-month, 340% surge to $15.71 on November 11, 2024, the day before the company released its 3Q24 financial report.

3Q24 Financial Report & FY24 Outlook

On November 12th, FreightCar posted earnings of $0.08 a share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of $10.9 million on revenue of $113.3 million, versus a loss of $0.12 a share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBITDA of $3.5 million on revenue of $61.9 million in 3Q23. The bottom line was $0.03 better than expectations.

That said, there were a number of troubling aspects to the report. First, although revenue was up 83% year-over-year, it was an eye-opening 19% shy of expectations. Management excused that to the timing of changeovers at the very end of the quarter, which prevented some deliveries in the final two weeks. Its conviction on this matter was demonstrated when FreightCar did not change its FY24 deliveries or revenue expectations, instead raising its Adj. EBITDA projection by $1 million to $38 million. Second, despite gaining significant market share in the quarter – characterized as 22% in the lines in which it participates ex-tank cars versus ~17% at the same time the year prior – management indicated that orders across the overall industry were “down roughly 20%”. This dynamic was reflected in the third issue, which was a 6% drop in backlog to 3,611 units ($372 million) despite the ~19% miss on deliveries.

Sprinkle in concern over Trump's potential tariffs, and a recipe for a 35% selloff to $10.17 a share in the subsequent trading session was in the offing. Shares of RAIL are now down a third since that 3Q24 report.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary

The other unresolved matter relates to the composition of FreightCar’s balance sheet. Although it holds no debt, the company issued preferred stock with a fair value of $85.4 million in May 2023 with an annual dividend that accrues at a rate of 17.5% and will grow by 0.5% per quarter on the fourth anniversary of its issuance. It is anticipated that management will recapitalize the balance sheet by entering into a significantly lower interest rate working capital facility and using the proceeds to retire the high interest preferred. Otherwise, FreightCar held cash of $44.8 million as of September 30, 2024.

The company’s rather dramatic turnaround has its one sell-side coverage with an opinion, Mark Reichman of Noble Capital Markets, bullish on its prospects, sporting a buy rating and a price objective of $15. Brendan McCarthy at Sidoti & Company shares the same price objective but does not have an official rating on shares of RAIL. On average, the two expect the company to earn $0.23 a share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $573.8 million in FY24, followed by $0.73 a share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $602.7 million in FY25.

After the severe reaction to the quarterly report, board member James Meyer purchased 23,400 shares of RAIL at $10.74 on November 14, 2024.

Verdict

The most concerning aspect of the FreightCar’s 3Q24 conference call commentary was the declaration that orders across the industry were slated to be down 20% in FY24. However, the industry is driven by the replacement cycle, as every railcar must be removed after 50 years in service. Competitor Trinity Industries (TRN) stated in August 2023 that ~250,000 rail cars would have to be retired in the next five years. Assuming that assessment is correct, a 100% replacement rate, and FreightCar’s exposure to 80% of the market, it would dictate that the company has good demand visibility over the ensuing four years. Also concerning, as mentioned, is the potential impact of tariffs.

That said, with an EV/FY24E Adj. EBITDA of under 8, a PE of just over 15 on FY25E EPS, and competencies now covering 80% of the total addressable railcar market, the company merits a small investment here with a more aggressive posture recommended below $9.00 or when the longer-term tariff policy towards Mexico crystallizes. I have accumulated a small stake in RAIL using covered call orders, it should be noted.

data.title: Alpha Picks Weekly Market Recap
data.description:
U.S. Federal Layoffs & Reciprocal Tariffs

The Trump administration’s decision to implement workforce reductions has significantly impacted federal employees, pushing them into a challenging job market. According to recent reports, approximately 75,000 federal workers agreed to deferred buyouts as part of an initiative led by the Trump administration to streamline government agencies. This move, aimed at reducing the size of the federal workforce, has sparked fears of increased unemployment and economic instability, particularly employees laid off find themselves struggling to secure new positions. Federal agencies must also collaborate with Elon Musk’s Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) to create plans to slash their workforces and hire only for “essential positions.” Career experts note that the broad experience possessed by these workers often doesn’t align with corporate job expectations, compounding their difficulty in transitioning into new roles. The administration’s actions not only highlight the volatile climate within federal employment but also raise concerns about the broader implications for regional labor markets and economic activity.

This week was full of events, including Trump’s formal announcement of the implementation of reciprocal tariffs, fulfilling a campaign promise to address trade imbalances: "Trump Reciprocal Trade Act." Trump also unveiled that he will add new automobile tariffs around April 2nd.

"If India, China, or any other country hits us with a 100 or 200 percent tariff on American-made goods, we will hit them with the same exact tariff. In other words, 100 percent is 100 percent. If they charge [the] US, we charge THEM—an eye for an eye, a tariff for a tariff, same exact amount…the only fair way to do it,” explained Trump.

This announcement marks a significant shift in U.S. trade policy, targeting countries with higher tariffs on American goods, like India and China. Trump’s “eye for an eye” tariff strategy involves matching foreign tariffs on U.S. exports, potentially escalating trade tensions. While the move aims to level the playing field for American businesses, it raises concerns about retaliatory measures from affected countries, which could disrupt global trade and economic stability. Despite the tariff tit-for-tat, stocks moved higher on Thursday, with the S&P 500 gaining +1.04%, the Nasdaq 1.5%, and the Dow +0.8%. The markets remain cautious as investors assess the potential impacts of these tariffs on international relations and economic growth.

Geopolitical Events

Geopolitical dynamics this week were significantly influenced by the anticipated easing of tensions between Ukraine and Russia. A prospective peace agreement uplifted regional markets, with Japan’s Nikkei surging 1.33% amid these hopes. Meanwhile, investors cautiously observed President Trump’s announcement of reciprocal tariffs, noting the potential implications for a broader trade conflict. The announcement risked unsettling global supply chains, particularly affecting sectors like energy, metals, and manufacturing, which could face inflated costs due to heightened duties.

Regionally, the geopolitical developments had mixed impacts. Despite burgeoning tariff fears, equity markets in Asia-Pacific reflected optimism over diplomatic engagements, with markets in Japan, India, and Hong Kong seeing gains partly attributed to robust earnings and supportive government policies. However, these gains contrast the more cautious tones in Europe and North America, where concerns about escalation in trade barriers pose potential headwinds to growth.

Macro Highlight: January’s U.S. Inflation Report

This past week, all eyes were on another hotly anticipated U.S. inflation report. CPI and core CPI clocked in at 3.0% and 3.3% unadjusted annualized rates in January 2025, respectively. Both results printed higher than economists’ expectations, with the core gauge surprising by an even wider margin.

Transportation services contributed to the price increase, where vehicle insurance ran at a hot +11.8% year-over-year rate (y/y). Used cars and trucks also rose +1.0% y/y after falling over 4% y/y in December 2024. Gasoline (all types) fell 0.2% y/y, much slower than the -3.3% print last month.

Following the inflation report, financial markets lowered interest rate cut expectations, pricing in just one cut from the Federal Reserve this year (versus two in late January). Since then, some of this dovish anticipation has been priced out, with Federal Funds Futures showing 1.5 cuts by the end of 2025.

GICS Sector Performance

This week saw notable movement across various sectors. Technology (XLK) and Consumer Staples (XLP) led the way, driven by strong earnings outlooks from major players like Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) and Alpha Picks stock, AppLovin Corporation (APP).

The tech sector was a top-performing sector this week

The Materials Sector (XLB) experienced an uptick despite fears of impending tariffs on imported resources, tempering some enthusiasm. Overall, the divergence highlights the continued influence of macroeconomic policy on sectorial performance within shifting geopolitical landscapes, as showcased in healthcare and financials experiencing weaker performance.

Value Stocks vs. Growth Stocks

Value and growth stocks were mixed, experiencing some fluctuations due to investor uncertainty around inflation, labor, macroeconomic stability, and trade tensions. These shifts highlight the importance of maintaining a balanced portfolio in times of market volatility.

Value and Growth Stocks were mixed this week

Small caps continue to trade at historically low valuations compared to many large caps. Despite any uncertainty, innovation and tech-driven stocks continue to be in high demand, as showcased by some of the 10 top small-cap stocks amid market turbulence.

Cryptocurrency

Cryptocurrencies experienced some volatility, with Bitcoin (BTC-USD) falling by 1.4% mid-week as investors mulled over regulatory challenges and macroeconomic variables, yet experienced an uptick during Friday trading. While enthusiasm for digital currencies remains, shifting global policies and economic uncertainties have weighed on market sentiment, reflecting a cautious investor approach toward digital assets in the current climate.

Coinbase Global (COIN) reported a significant boost in its Q4 earnings, exceeding market forecasts. Its financial performance underscores the growing appeal and profitability of cryptocurrency trading platforms as digital currency adoption gains in popularity. However, despite these positive numbers, the broader crypto market remains wary amid persistent regulatory challenges. These developments highlight the complexities Coinbase faces as it navigates a rapidly evolving financial landscape, balancing substantial growth opportunities against potential legal and compliance hurdles.

Currency

Despite recent setbacks, the US dollar maintained a long-term positive outlook, even as it dropped 0.7% against major market peers this week. The decline can be attributed to diminishing rate cut prospects and other central banks moving forward with their easing policies.

The Japanese Yen continued to rally.

The Japanese yen continued to show strength, gaining against the U.S. dollar, influenced by heightened safe-haven appeal amid global economic uncertainties. This strengthening of the yen can be attributed to geopolitical developments, such as the anticipated easing of tensions between Ukraine and Russia, and ongoing trade discussions stemming from the newly announced reciprocal tariffs by the Trump administration. These factors have led investors to seek stability in the yen, anticipating potential fluctuations in other major currencies that are more directly impacted by trade negotiations and geopolitical events. Despite this rally, market analysts remain cautious about the yen’s longer-term trajectory, given the complex interplay of monetary policies and international relations that continue to shape currency markets.

Charts of the Week - Gold Continues its Remarkable Run Higher

Gold climbed ~2% this week, earning another gain and a seventh consecutive weekly increase. This was last witnessed during the global pandemic in 2020. Since late 1979, there have been 1,229 positive weeks for the yellow metal; 7-week wins occurred 12 times, a mere 1% of the sample.

To this day, the United States remains the world’s largest holder of gold reserves, with nearly 8.1k metric tons. Germany, Italy, and France follow at 3.4k, 2.5k, and 2.4k, respectively. According to The Economist, central banks have been building up their gold reserves, which rose to 11% as a percentage of the total in 2023—the highest in over 20 years.

Some argue that this demand helped weaken gold’s relationship with interest rates. The yellow metal produces no income compared to other investment instruments, making its surge remarkable given that interest rates remain relatively high compared to pre-Covid.

For a potential “golden opportunity” and portfolio diversification, consider some Top Gold or Commodity ETFs. Alternatively, Alpha Picks offers a diversified portfolio of stock picks using Seeking Alpha’s data-driven tools used to identify the best-of-the-best ‘Strong Buy’ stocks.

Alpha Picks

What is Alpha Picks? Alpha Picks provides two data-driven stock picks a month, delivered straight to your inbox. Alpha Picks has collectively gained 178%, compared to the S&P 500’s 62%.

What has Alpha Picks done lately? Just in time for Valentine’s Day, Alpha Picks stock AppLovin Corporation delivered an incredible performance for the portfolio. AppLovin is an AI-powered advertising tech company and was one of this week’s biggest stock movers, rallying over 30% after beating Q4 2024 EPS and revenue estimates. It has returned over 1000% to the Alpha Picks portfolio since its addition on November 15, 2023.

Alpha Picks has been resilient amid market volatility and geopolitical tensions, with the majority exceeding earnings expectations and beating revenue targets. If you want to get in on the action, the next Alpha Pick will be released on February 18th.

Economic Rundown

► FOMC – The Federal Reserve held rates steady at 4.25%–4.50% during its January 29th meeting, focusing on emerging inflation trends ahead of its March meeting, where future policy direction will be reassessed. Markets are not anticipating a rate cut until at least June 2025.

► Inflation— January’s CPI report showing a 3% YoY rise underscores persistent inflationary pressures, challenging the Fed’s 2% target and demanding vigilance in monetary policy adjustments.

► Labor Market – Recent employment data has reflected modest improvements, with the U.S. economy adding 143,000 jobs in January. But with inflation concerns persisting, stakeholders are keenly awaiting the next employment report to gauge labor market resilience amidst macroeconomic shifts and the administration’s ongoing federal worker layoff impacts. Nearly 75,000 employees accepted the buyout, prompting concerns about increased governmental disruptions and potential impacts on regional labor markets.

data.title: Arm Holdings: Severe Overvaluation And Weaker Market Outlook
data.description:
By Robert Clark

Arm Holdings (NASDAQ:ARM) reported its Q3 FY25 earnings on Feb 5, 2025 and the stock tanked over 7% on weaker royalty outlook.

The headline numbers from ARM's Q3 FY25 earnings are solid at first glance, with total revenue up 19% YoY to $983 million. Royalty revenue was up 23% and totaled $580 million, while licensing revenue was up 14% at $403 million. However, I think that these growth rates are unlikely to be sustainable and that there are some warning signs below the surface.

Most concerning is the flatlining of ARM's all-important Armv9 adoption rate at 25% of royalty revenue for three consecutive quarters. They attempted to put a positive spin on this flatlining, saying it provides a "runway for future growth." But I don't believe so. That companies aren't actually adopting the new product probably means that major customers aren't interested and that may be a sign of broader problems with how they are handling this whole product transition process.

KeyBanc analysts said ARM's 23% y/y royalty revenue growth will not be sustainable. A lot of the growth was from non-recurring benefits, like MediaTek adopting CSS for the first time in their D9400 mobile platform and server CPU shipments like AWS Graviton and Google Axion.

These gave a short-term boost, but it does not always equate to sustained growth. And besides, ARM's own guidance is that royalty revenues may actually drop next quarter thanks to the normal seasonal weakening of the smartphone market and possibly weaker IoT revenues.

ARM's dependence on the smartphone market is particularly worrisome given structural challenges facing this market. Global smartphone sales are 19% lower than their 2017 peak, and second-hand phones now represent 22% of the market, a staggering rise from just 4% in 2014. The growth in the used smartphone market directly affects ARM's royalty revenue prospects in that there are fewer new phones, meaning less scope for premium Armv9 adoption.

Qualcomm's (QCOM) continuing use of Armv8-based Nuvia CPUs can also slow down Armv9 adoption rates even more. Huawei has shown strong growth in China through the development of its own chip designs and operating system, providing a template for other manufacturers to reduce their usage of ARM technology.

ARM is a solid company with great technology and a solid market position. Nevertheless, I'm starting coverage with a SELL rating due to a number of reasons. Adoption of its products has stalled, there are structural market headwinds, competition is getting fiercer and the valuation is too rich. All of these mean the stock price can fall significantly from current levels.

Extreme Overvaluation and Market Headwinds

ARM's current P/S ratio of 40.98x is almost 1182% higher than the semiconductor IP companies and its sector average.

This basically means the stock price is valued at over 41 times the projected annual revenue. Investors are paying a whopping $41 for every $1 of projected future revenue that's a lot of trust.

Historically, semiconductor companies usually trade at forward P/S multiples between 5-8x and even those high growth software companies rarely stay above 20x for long. ARM's 40.98x forward P/S ratio is way above the industry averages.

For this to make sense, ARM would need to either achieve crazy high and continuous revenue growth while keeping the current margins or significantly expand its operating margins.

However, considering ARM's cost structure, especially its rising R&D and operational expenses, margin expansion is highly improbable.

So, if ARM’s P/S ratio were to drop to an industry average 8x, how long would investors need to wait for revenue to catch up?

Let’s break it down:

  • Annual Revenue FY24: $3.2 billion

  • Current Forward P/S Ratio: 40.98x

  • Target P/S Ratio (Industry Average): 8x

  • Market Cap (at 40.98x P/S): $163 billion

  • Revenue Growth Rate Assumption: 14.5% annually

Note that I use 14.5% rate because ARM’s revenue CAGR is 14.5% from 2021 to 2025(e).

At a 14.5% revenue growth rate, ARM would take over 13 years to organically come down to an 8x P/S valuation at today’s market cap. This means that unless ARM dramatically accelerates its revenue growth or the stock price corrects downward, investors at current levels are betting on 13 years of sustained expansion to justify today’s valuation.

If you look at their guidance, the Q4 FY25 revenue guidance is $1.17 billion to $1.27 billion, 32% y/y growth at the midpoint. But they have widened their guidance range. It seems to me that the widened guidance range might suggest a lack of confidence in securing major new licensing deals.

ARM's largest end-market, the smartphone market, is experiencing structural problems that are not adequately reflected in the ARM valuation. Global smartphone sales have fallen 19% from their 2017 high and have no clear signs of recovery.

The used smartphone market has grown from 4% of total smartphone sales in 2014 to 22% in 2024, representing 348 million units. This directly impacts ARM's royalty revenue as they only get royalties from the sale of new devices.

The industry has tried again and again to drive growth with innovation, but it failed. Foldable phones, which were previously touted as the next growth driver, have seen decreasing demand despite huge hype and promotion around them. Vendors have already begun to shift R&D investments away from foldables since people are not interested in them.

Competition in ARM's key markets is heating up. Huawei's comeback in China is evidence of this. In the absence of U.S. chips, Huawei built its own chips and operating system, Harmony OS Next. Huawei sales in 2024 jumped 37% to take number two in China and push Apple down to number three. This is evidence that local players can prevail in their home markets. One of the largest worries is the sluggish adoption of Armv9. Despite the fact that management had previously guided for 60 to 70% penetration, the adoption has stalled at 25% over the past three quarters. This is even as Qualcomm continues to use Armv8 Nuvia CPUs, which suggests that important clients may be reluctant to switch.

While management is optimistic regarding Compute Subsystems (CSS) adoption, CSS revenue contribution remains minimal. The lengthy development cycles in implementing CSS solutions may delay any substantial revenue contribution further. In addition, management has mentioned that CSS royalty rates can fluctuate year over year, which would make their revenue guidance noisier.

ARM is in a difficult position right now and there are several things that are threatening their top dog status.

The new regional chip projects, new chip architectures like RISC-V, big tech companies creating their own chips and countries wanting to be chip independent. All this, combined with a saturated market and tech adoption issues, makes ARM's 40.98x P/S ratio look much too high. I think that the share price has a strong likelihood of imploding.

Why I Could Be Wrong

Although the current situation leads me to a bearish conclusion regarding ARM, I would not recommend shorting the stock. I just think that it is a good idea to get out of the position rather than be in it.

ARM's CEO, Rene Haas, said on the earnings call that AI workloads will need to run everywhere, from the lowest powered devices up to the highest powered data centers. The potential catalyst that can work against my bearish thesis is the rapid adoption of AI across computing platforms.

ARM's partnership with NVIDIA (NVDA) on Project DIGITS with the fusion of the ARM-based Grace CPU with NVIDIA's Blackwell GPU in the GB10 Superchip shows the company's potential in high performance AI computing. The Grace CPU is well positioned for the AI data center transition since it has included 20 power-efficient ARM cores comprising 10 Cortex-X925 and 10 Cortex-A725.

Arm said in their Q3 shareholder letter that on AWS, more than half of all new CPU capacity installed in the past two years are on ARM-based Graviton, and over 90% of AWS's largest 1000 EC2 customers are using Graviton. This suggests growing momentum for ARM in the cloud computing space.

One other possible advantage that could continue to drive ARM even further is if they keep executing in new markets. Their Compute Subsystems (CSS) strategy is already executing better than expected, and they already have 12 licensees on board. If CSS takes hold in the automotive sector, that could translate to a lot more revenue for ARM. Management commented on the earnings call that they're still grinding away at the automotive CSS efforts, so there's definitely potential for even more growth further down the line that we haven't yet seen.

While these potential positives do not alter my overall bearish thesis at present valuation, they are important variables that can result in better than expected performance.

To Sum Up

ARM's valuation currently is way too rich, and a more reasonable valuation would be 25 to 30x sales. Hence, there is a high risk of the price dropping at current levels.

Investors must keep a close eye on Armv9 adoption rates and smartphone market trends, which could be the key pointers to revising the thesis. With the sky-high valuation, market headwinds and decelerating adoption metrics, the risk/reward profile at current levels does not appear attractive. While ARM is still a great company with excellent technology, the current price has a downside risk that's too great.

data.title: Keurig Dr Pepper: Trying To Win The Beverage Wars In 2025
data.description:
The Company Behind Iconic Drink Brands In US Supermarkets

In celebration of my birth year, 1981, today's article covers a company that Seeking Alpha says was founded that year in the consumer staples sector, Keurig Dr Pepper (NASDAQ:KDP).

As a dividend investor, it is also newsworthy that the company declared a dividend a few days ago on Feb. 11th, and its next earnings results are due in a few weeks on Feb. 25th so this article serves as a pre-earnings preview to get a better idea about this stock.

Although its early 1980s roots are tied mostly to Green Mountain Coffee Roasters, due to mergers in 2018, the current company evolved into an umbrella for many brands focusing heavily on drinks, including the iconic American brand Dr. Pepper, which has much older roots in Waco, Texas.

The goal of today's article is to explore multiple data points and angles to get a more holistic view of the potential upside and downside factors of this stock, ahead of its next earnings call, as well as what to possibly expect in the next results.

Thesis: Bullish Call Ahead of Q4 Earnings Results

For my first-ever rating of this stock, my thesis will argue for a buy rating.

To summarize the upside factors considered, this company has a proven strong profit margin and diversified product portfolio driven by recognizable brands, a low debt risk with investment-grade credit rating, proven dividend growth, and further growth potential after acquiring Ghost energy beverages.

The downside for this stock could come from 2025 coffee price inflation impacting margins in their coffee pod business segment, the stock now trading at elevated multiples, and the potential for consumers to not response favorably to the new flavor line the company has invested so much in lately.

I will go into each of these in the following sections, providing supporting evidence along the way.

In An Inflationary Macro Environment, Brand Loyalty And Product Diversification Could Lower Risk

To first get a sense of the larger macro picture impacting the consumer staples sector and its future, here is what a late-December article in Nasdaq had to say:

As we enter 2025, the consumer staples sector remains a cornerstone of economic stability and growth. These companies, producing everyday essentials, have a strong presence in global markets, regardless of economic fluctuations.

More recently, though, an early-January research paper by global consultancy Deloitte highlighted that continued elevated inflation could drive a more selective customer:

Though inflation is returning to more normal levels, prices remain elevated and consumers aren’t experiencing much relief. Consumer products companies may find consumers trading down, finding substitutes, or exiting categories altogether.

Another macro factor to consider, since this company is also in the coffee pod segment, is projected rising prices of coffee, which I believe could increase input costs.

For example, this week's article in Business Insider highlighted that "Bank of America says coffee is likely to get more expensive in 2025 amid weak crop yields."

"In a note on Monday, the analysts pointed to the growing shortage of arabica beans. Arabica coffee prices closed at an all-time high of $4.04 a pound on Friday, up by 26% since the fourth quarter," the article continued.

I think that what could help consumer staples firms like Keurig Dr Pepper is a robust product and geographic diversification across its portfolio.

For example, it seems to achieve this when looking at the net sales growth from FY24 Q3 results below, showing that although the US coffee segment saw a sales decline, it was offset by gains in US refreshments and intl segments:

Further evidence of robust product diversification can be seen from data on the company website, indicating this company covers several grocery categories including ready-to-brew products, ready-to-drink, and ready-to-eat.

In fact, those of us who grew up with some of these brands during visits to the local supermarket know that customer brand loyalty can be a valuable driver of sales. For instance, Mott's apple sauce would be in our school lunch box in the early 1990s, and today in 2025 it remains of this company's key brands.

For the reasons above, I believe longstanding brand loyalty and a diverse product portfolio lessens the downside risk for this firm, even in an inflationary environment.

Positive Top-Line Outlook And New Brand Acquisition To Grow Portfolio

In this section, I will talk somewhat about future growth drivers.

Notable to mention is that Seeking Alpha data shows a fwd revenue growth estimate of 4.7%, well above the sector average hovering near 2%.

Growth of new brands through acquisition can also be a future revenue driver, and the acquisition of energy drink brand GHOST could add growth to this company's portfolio.

In fact, in Q3 results press release comments the CEO said:

This morning’s exciting announcement of our acquisition of GHOST is yet another such step, accelerating our portfolio evolution toward growth-accretive and consumer-preferred spaces.

Further, I think this could increase this company's market share in the energy drinks niche.

The company in Q3 comments had a positive outlook for FY24, and "reaffirmed its fiscal 2024 guidance for constant currency net sales growth in a mid-single-digit range and Adjusted diluted EPS growth in a high-single-digit range."

Strong Gross And Net Profit Margins, But Keeping Eye on 2025 Impact From Coffee Prices

When it comes to what could continue to drive a healthy bottom line in 2025, I believe in considering the strong profit margin.

For example, when compared to peers in this space such as Monster Beverage (MNST), PepsiCo (PEP), and Coca-Cola (KO), the 55.6% gross profit margin of this company is on the higher end of this peer group and therefore competitive.

As far as net income margin, the bottom line, peer data shows that Keurig Dr Pepper has a margin of 15%, around 3rd in this peer group:

However, compared to the overall sector which has a net income margin of closer to 4%, this stock easily beats the sector.

Looking ahead, though, expected increased market costs of commodities like coffee, which I mentioned earlier, could impact the margin in that category this year. In Q3 results, for example, although the US refreshment beverages saw growth in operating income, it was also "partially offset by the impact of cost inflation."

Therefore, while the strong profit margin could drive future upside, I also think the impact of coffee price inflation could drive some downside in that part of the business.

A Steady Positive Cashflow Generator

Considering this company achieved positive free cashflow/share in 5 of the last 6 quarters reported, a notable item to mention is that this company seems like a strong cashflow generator.

I believe a strong driver of that is the cash from operations showing a trend of being steadily positive and lately generating over +$600MM from core business operations:

A combination of steady profitability and positive cashflow generation, if it continues, should provide justification to support the dividend going forward.

A Low Debt/Equity Along With Investment-Grade Rating

The good news to report about this company is that its debt/equity ratio is just 0.59, relatively on the lower end of the following peer group:

The balance sheet remains strong, with over +$24B in positive equity.

Further, back in spring 2024 ratings agency S&P reaffirmed a "BBB" credit rating on this company, which is considered investment-grade, and had the following positive sentiment:

We expect KDP will continue to expand its EBITDA margins on its ongoing realization of operating efficiencies, lower input-cost inflation, and a shift in its sales mix toward its higher-margin offerings.

Therefore, when considering both its debt risk and credit rating, I think the upside is there as this type of lower-risk company will be better positioned to get more favorable financing in the near future if needed.

The Dividend Appeal Is Focused on Growth, Not Yield

Towards the start of the article, I mentioned the company declared a dividend again recently, so this section takes a quick look at the dividend case for this stock.

The yield of around 2.8% is not too remarkable, considering its sector average is around 3%. It would not be on my list of stocks to add to a high-yield portfolio, of course.

More positive is the 5-year dividend growth CAGR at 8%, easily beating the sector average and proving this company has been committed to returning cash back to shareholders.

In order for that trend to continue, as mentioned earlier, the positive cashflow will have to continue as well as earnings growth. I believe the strong profit margins will help in this, and the fact that it has a low debt burden, so more cash will be available for dividends.

Despite Price Dip, Stock Remains Overvalued vs Sector

In this section, let's talk about the share price and valuation, to get an idea if it helps the case for a buy, hold, or sell.

The following chart I made compares the share price (at market close on Feb. 14th) vs its 200-day SMA and 5 year min/max, and we can see it closed at 6.8% below the 200-day average.

What if this is presenting a dip-buying opportunity now?

That may appear so from just the chart alone, however let's also consider valuation too, and two good metrics for future-minded investors would be the forward P/E ratio and forward EV/EBITDA compared to the sector.

From Seeking Alpha data, this stock's fwd P/E (GAAP) shows this stock trading at a 19.3x forward earnings multiple, even though the sector average is just under 18x.

The fwd EV/EBITDA is a 13x multiple vs the sector being just under 10x.

I believe this elevated valuation is driven by a combination of factors: the Q3 results showed YoY revenue and earnings growth achieved and a positive FY guidance, the analyst consensus pointing to YoY EPS growth for FY25, as well as the strong profit margin I mentioned.

So, 2 weeks before the next earnings result, I think despite the overvaluation (which seems justified) buying the dip now could prove positive if the firm meets or beats expectations, but not so much if they have an earnings miss.

Incidentally, in terms of fwd P/E valuation, this peer comparison shows that Keurig Dr Pepper is actually trading at a lower multiple than beverage peers Coca-Cola and Monster Beverages:

For this reason, I would consider my entire thesis and not just price and valuation alone, since sometimes the valuation picture by itself may be presenting a mixed bag. Nevertheless, this elevated valuation may turn away those value-driven investors eager to scoop up a deal.

Additional Risk: Consumer Response To New Products

In this section, I will touch on an additional risk not mentioned so far elsewhere in the article.

In early February, the company announced (as per PR Newswire) that they are rolling out an "exciting 2025 new flavor lineup in the U.S., featuring beloved brands such as Dr Pepper, 7UP, A&W and Snapple."

For example, they are rolling out "7Up Tropical" and "Dr Pepper Blackberry" as two of the new flavors, in a push to innovate on their existing cold beverage portfolio.

This effort, which clearly had a cost in terms of flavor research and development, along with new branding, could present both an upside and downside risk.

The upside could come from a really positive consumer response, which translates to increased sales over an extended period. However, a negative consumer response could present a downside and eventually the new products getting pulled.

Consider, for example, a Sept. 2024 article in Newsweek about Coca-Cola cancelling its new "Coca-Cola Spiced" flavor after just 7 months on the market.

According to the article, "national interest appeared to wane quickly, leading to speculation about the flavor's viability."

In addition, one of the key peers/competitors in the beverage space I mentioned earlier is PepsiCo, and they too are competing for customer tastebuds by launching a new Pepsi flavor called "Wild Cherry & Cream", as reported in USAToday in January.

So, competition from big players like that should remind investors of this stock that the beverage wars in 2025 are just starting to heat up and Keurig Dr Pepper is not the only one launching new flavors.

Conclusion: A Dip-Buying Opportunity Ahead of Next Earnings

To conclude this paper, below is my summary rating sheet showing all the upside and downside factors I presented today, and the weight they gave to the total upside or downside rating score:

In this case, the upside potential for this stock is far higher than the downside risk, so I gave it a buy rating, which agrees with today's consensus from Wall St and the SA quant system.

This could be an opportunity to snatch up some shares of this stock while it is on a price dip, ahead of the next earnings call, since I am more convinced they will meet or beat expectations and pull up the share price.

This stock will also be included in my dividend ideas for the week.

Wrap Up

This article is part of my ongoing theme for 2025 on this platform called Financial Markets: Growing A Dividend Income Portfolio.

I welcome reader comments on the question: where do you see the consumer staples sector going in 2025 and why?

data.title: ASML: 2025 Guidance May Have Overlooked An Unlikely But Significant Event
data.description:
ASML Holding N.V. (NASDAQ:ASML) has seen its share price decline by over 30% since its all-time highs in July last year, significantly underperforming the semiconductor industry and the S&P 500.

From what I am reading in the latest SA articles about this stock, most traditional value investors are celebrating the discount like it's Christmas morning.

I'm sorry to be the Grinch, but I believe readers are overlooking the downside risk of owning this stock. To me, the risk/reward ratio is not skewed to the upside. I will hint in this introduction that it relates to the rivalry between the US (including its allies) and China in the AI race. I believe one specific event might not be included in the 2025 revenue guidance or the expected revenue normalization from China that management mentioned in Q3 last year and reaffirmed in Q4.

Just to be clear. I am not talking here about the AI race for consumer applications in the likes of ChatGPT or DeepSeek. Right now, I couldn't be less worried about AI consumer applications in China. What makes me sweat (and I believe it does the same to the US and its allies) is China’s advancements in AI for military applications. I believe this is the main reason behind the recent push to slow down China’s progress in the semiconductor industry.

I believe these efforts will only intensify in 2025. Therefore, I rate ASML as a strong sell, with a short-term timeframe in mind of approximately 12 months.

The Bear Case

The release of Q2 2024 earnings marked the start of a downturn in investor confidence. Back then, the company surpassed EPS and revenue expectations by over 8% and 3%, respectively. However, the first chip in the glass appeared when management projected Q3 2024 total net sales between €6.7 billion and €7.3 billion, falling short of the Street’s expectation of €7.6 billion. This, along with rumors that surfaced just a day before earnings, triggered a panic selloff. Since then, the share price dropped by 40%, hitting its lowest point in mid-November last year, just weeks after the company accidentally released its Q3 results one day early. Yet again, results in Q3 surpassed The Street's expectations, but the stock experienced the biggest daily decline since its IPO, after reporting an order intake of €2.6 billion vs the anticipated €5 billion and lowering 2025 revenue guidance from €30B- €40B down to €30B - €35B. The small chip in the mirror from Q2 now became a visible crack.

At the moment of writing this article, the share price is still over 30% below the ATH from July 2024. For the reasons I discuss below, I believe the company has a low chance to turn around the investor sentiment in 2025.

First, the lion's share of the 2024 revenue came from system sales, which accounted for €21.8 billion. Within this segment, China was the main revenue contributor, responsible for 41% of net sales.

To put things into perspective, in 2023, China was responsible for just 29% of this segment's revenue, falling behind Taiwan's 30%.

During the Q3 earnings call, management already hinted at a "normalization" of the sales in China during 2025.

I think we all read newspapers, right? We all see that there is speculation around export controls. And also that is a driver for us to take a more cautious view on the China sales. So with that combined, we stated that we believe the China sales for next year are going to go to, let's say, 20% of our expected sales level for next year.

This normalization is baked into the latest revenue guidance for 2025.

Now, what if the actual contribution from China in 2025 is less than the historical 20%? What if the export control regulations tighten in 2025, particularly as the Trump administration pushes its allies (the Netherlands, in this case) for even tighter restrictions?

Back in 2018, the Dutch government granted ASML an export license to sell advanced EUV tools to a Chinese customer. However, in 2019, before any of the EUV tools were delivered, the US under the Trump administration persuaded the Dutch Prime Minister to not renew ASML's license. Needless to say, they succeeded and ASML lost its export license for EUV tools in 2019.

Back in 2023, the Dutch government required an export license for the NXT:2000 series (a DUV tool), and more recently in 2024, they required an export license for the 1970i and 1980i DUV models. Just a month ago, the Dutch government announced a new rule requiring companies to get export licenses for measuring and inspection equipment. This includes technologies used to detect tiny defects in wafers and systems that improve measurements after processes like deposition and etching. As I said before, all this is already baked into the guidance and analyst expectations for FY 2025.

So, here is my bear case: seeing the increased export control licenses for the DUV machines over the last 18 months, and considering the new administration in the US and their push to curb China's access to advanced semiconductor manufacturing equipment, I believe there is a high risk for the DUV export license to be revoked by the Dutch prime minister if the US escalates the curbing measure to China. I have a high conviction that this event is not baked into the guidance. If this happens, the revenue normalization in China could be significantly lower than the 20% expectation. As to how lower, no one knows due to dual-use export control regulations, which don't allow ASML to break down the revenue per product in that country.

Given that expectations are already low, any reduction in the current guidance could push the share price even lower. Mid $500s could be a price target for the rock bottom if you look at the next solid support on a weekly chart.

What is the chance of this event (i.e. ASML losing its DUV export control license to sell these tools in China)? I don't know. No one does. But the risk it carries is significant to make me not consider this stock in my portfolio (and as you may know, I love high risk).

Certainly, what I care about the most is not how probable an event is, but what's the payoff. If you are betting on a turnaround (let's say you believe the share price will retest ATHs in the $1,100s), are you able to stomach a potential 25% decline in the share price and hold on to your position until investor sentiment changes (which could take several years)? Is this downside worth a 50% return? For me, the answer to this question is a clear no. Nonetheless, I am mindful that other investors could have different timeframes and strategies in mind.

Right now, I don't see an asymmetric risk/reward ratio of owning shares in this stock. The export control restrictions coming from the US can only intensify, considering the rivalry with China.

Overall, I believe in America. I believe the country will do whatever it takes to succeed in the AI race. I believe they will increase the pressure on its allies to do the same. This is simply the reason I am bearish on ASML in the short term.

How I Can Be Wrong

My thesis could take longer than expected to materialize. This could happen if the US is no longer pushing for additional export control requirements affecting ASML's exports in China, or if the Dutch PM refuses to implement the suggested restrictions by the US government.

In the meantime, shares could see price appreciation just because they are trading at attractive valuation ratios when compared to the historical range. As I said in my Palantir (PLTR) article, people love buying the dips (contrary to what Buffett says). Therefore, in the short term, we could see shares trading back in the $800s and even in the $900s, as The Street is expecting.

Another factor that could support a bull case is the recent decision to stop reporting quarterly bookings after 2025. Due to the nature of the business, and considering that one of ASML's most advanced tools costs several hundred million USD (like the High-NA Twinscan EXE lithography machines, priced over $380 million), I believe this decision was wise. However, the company is still exposed to irrational panic selloff risks in 2025 as they will still report order bookings until the end of the year (coincidentally, until I believe my strong sell rating will be valid).

Speaking of high NA EUV systems, in the past quarter, ASML successfully installed and secured customer acceptance for two of these tools. A third high NA EUV system was shipped in Q4 2024. Even though no high NA revenue recognition is expected in Q1 2025 (which is positive for the overall gross margins), I believe 2026 will see a major increase in revenue from this tool as customers move from R&D and process validation to full high volume production. Again, my bear thesis has a timeframe of 12 months, just before revenue starts to ramp up from the high NA EUV machines.

Conclusion

To wrap up, I believe holding ASML shares presents an asymmetric risk/reward ratio. Therefore, I believe this stock is a Strong Sell considering a short-term investment timeframe of 12 months.

To be specific, I believe the rivalry between the US and China will escalate in 2025, leading to the Trump administration pushing its allies to increase export control regulations, potentially leading to the Dutch PM revoking some of the licenses granted to ASML over the past 18 months. Among these export licenses, ASML's 1970i, 1980i, and the NXT:2000 DUV tools could be at risk, particularly the latter one as it is the most advanced.

I believe the revenue guidance for FY 2025 doesn't take into account the small, but real chance of losing DUV export controls.

I'll be upfront, this is highly speculative, and the share price could appreciate in the short term. However, the significant downside risk would definitely keep me up at night if I owned shares in this company.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Coherent: Growth Is Supported By Accelerating AI-Infrastructure Investments
data.description:
Investment action

I recommended a buy rating for Coherent Corp. (NYSE:COHR) when I wrote about it in October 2023, as I expected its datacom segment to be a key growth driver, supported by demand for AI. The stock has done really well since my coverage, now up ~240% since then. Based on my current outlook and analysis, I reiterate a buy rating. I believe the growth outlook remains robust as the pace of AI infrastructure investments does not appear to be slowing down anytime soon. On the contrary, it could very well continue to accelerate for the foreseeable future.

Review

COHR reported 2Q25 earnings earlier in February, delivering revenue of $1.435 billion, representing a growth of 27% y/y and 6% sequentially. Datacom led the strong performance, growing 79% y/y as AI hyperscaler demand for 800G transceivers remained strong. Meanwhile, the telecom segment did well too, delivering 16% sequential and 11% y/y growth. Margins also showed meaningful improvement, with gross margin reaching 38.2%, up 146 bps sequentially and 363 bps y/y, which led to EBIT margin expanding to 18.5%, a substantial jump from 13.5% in 2Q24. On the bottom line, adj EPS saw $0.95, which was well above consensus estimates of $0.69. Overall, this quarter marks a clear inflection point for COHR, in that revenue growth is translating into solid earnings growth.

I remain optimistic about COHR's growth runway. Datacom remains CHOR's strongest growth driver, with 79% y/y revenue growth in 2Q25, and I expect this momentum to continue. The main driver is the rapid advancement of AI, which has led to a significant increase in the complexity of AI models. For readers that are unaware, complexity increases because of the need to enhance performance across various applications. A notable example is the scaling of AI models. The idea is that by increasing the size of models and the amount of training data, it should lead to improved performance. Consequently, it requires substantially more computational resources to train these models (grows exponentially). Looking ahead, third-party estimates suggest that AI models could be scaled up significantly by 2030, potentially increasing in size by up to 10,000 times compared to current models.

This entire growth cycle heavily benefits COHR's datacom segment, and the surge in 800G transceiver shipments highlights the sustained demand from hyperscalers (hyperscalers are the vast majority of COHR’s Datacom customers) as they expand AI training and inference workloads. Importantly, management confirmed that 1.6T transceivers remain on track for initial revenue contributions in 2025, signaling a longer runway for growth beyond the current 800G cycle. One important takeaway here is that management's ongoing customer evaluations for 1.6T transceivers suggest a smooth transition to the next technology cycle without disrupting demand for 800G, so there should not be any “air pockets” of sales growth decelerating.

In 2Q25, COHR has tripled its indium phosphide [InP] output y/y, and I think this expansion of in-house InP capacity is a critical move (InP is a key enabler of both EML (Electro-Absorption Modulated Laser) and CW (Continuous Wave) lasers, which power high-speed optical transmission in AI data centers), as it ensures COHR maintains supply chain control and cost advantages over competitors relying on third-party fabs. There shouldn’t be issues for COHR to further expand capacity, as it is also benefiting from US government support (as noted in the earnings), with CHIPS Act funding earmarked for expanding its Sherman, Texas, facility.

Telecom Recovery Gaining Momentum

On the other hand, the telecom segment recovery adds another layer of upside. Revenue in the segment increased 16% sequentially and 11% y/y in 2Q25, marking two consecutive quarters of growth. Management even upgraded its outlook from "cautious" to "cautiously optimistic," which I take as a reflection of improvement in underlying demand. A key driver of telecom’s recovery is the ramp of new products, including 100G, 400G, and 800G ZR/ZR+ coherent transceivers, which saw solid demand in 2Q25. My thoughts are that the benefits from the AI-infrastructure investment cycle have spilled into the telecom industry. Specifically, AI workloads are generating increasing traffic between data centers, which requires the telecom industry to step up investments for higher-capacity interconnects, which drives up investment in optical transport networks.

Valuation

Despite the strong rally over the past year, I think COHR is still undervalued. As of the time of writing, COHR's forward EBITDA multiple is 13.5x, while Ciena Corp. (CIEN) trades at 20.6x (I switched my comp set because Datacom is going to be the main growth driver ahead). I think COHR should trade closer to CIEN, given that both companies have similar expected growth (per consensus) or mid-teens over the next 12 months, but COHR has double the adj EBITDA margin (24% as of 2Q25 vs. CIEN’s 12.2% as of 4Q24). Even if I were to assume that the higher leverage ratio that COHR has deserves a discount (3.3x net debt to EBITDA vs. CIEN’s 1.3x), I think a ~7x discount is too much. I think the market will eventually wake up and realize that this discount is not justified, and COHR will trade up.

Risk

Datacom revenue growth could slow if hyperscalers delay AI infrastructure spending. Although I am confident that the overall trajectory will not change, the timing of investments and COHR recognizing revenue could be lumpy. There could be periods of sharp growth deceleration that may cause investors to worry. Additionally, geopolitical risks, especially with regard to the US and China, and potential tariffs may disrupt supply chains, which impacts COHR costs and ability to meet demand.

Final thoughts

My recommendation is a buy rating for COHR. The rapid development in the AI industry has paved a solid growth runway for COHR for the coming years. So long as investments in AI infrastructure continue, COHR should have no issues growing its datacom segment at a rapid pace. Eventually, the market should realize that COHR should not trade at such a big discount to CIEN, and a positive re-rating should happen.
